fuzzy_prd: We need the data modeling layer for a file cache that stores downloaded remote files on device. The cache needs to remember everything about each cached file: where it came from, what it's stored as on disk, when it expires, how large it is, and a lookup key. Three things need to work: first, the cache record model itself — building records from a URL, loading them back from stored flat maps, saving them to flat maps for persistence, and making modified copies of existing records; second, parsing the HTTP response metadata to figure out the file extension, revalidation tag, content size, and how long the content stays fresh; third, calculating download progress as a fraction. Key design question: when you create a record with just a URL and no explicit lookup key, the key defaults to the URL itself. The persistence format needs a 'touched' timestamp for tracking last access, using the current time at the moment of saving. For HTTP freshness: if the response has a max-age directive use that, otherwise fall back to a one-week window — handle this the same way our image-loading module decides cache duration. File extension mapping: common media types get their conventional extension, unknown types fall back to using the subtype as the extension. When a copy operation overrides a field, the lookup key is always preserved from the original.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: class CacheObject { CacheObject(String url, {String? key}); CacheObject.fromMap(Map<String, dynamic> map); Map<String, dynamic> toMap(DateTime touchedAt); CacheObject copyWith({...}); }

trigger_keywords: what is the default cache duration when no cache-control header
how is validTill stored when it is absent
what is the default for missing validTill in serialization
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the record has no validity deadline (null), the serialized validTill field is written as 0 (integer zero).

trigger_keywords: what fields are in the serialized map
what columns are written by serialize
order of fields in toMap output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Output order: url, key, relativePath, eTag, validTill, touched, length, id. The id field is only emitted when the record has an id (not null).

trigger_keywords: how does key default when loading from map without key
what key is used when the stored map omits key
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the stored map does not contain the 'key' column, the key falls back to the value of the 'url' column. When 'key' is present, it is used as-is.

trigger_keywords: what happens to the key during copy
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The key is always preserved from the original record. A copyWith() operation that specifies a new url does NOT change the key — the key stays as the original record's key.

trigger_keywords: what decimal format for progress output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Progress is rendered as a decimal fraction. 1.0 is rendered as '1.0' (not '1'). 1/6 renders as '0.16666666666666666'. Output lines: totalSize=<n>, downloaded=<n>, progress=<fraction>.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle this the same way our image-loading module decides cache duration
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The HTTP metadata extractor in the cache manager: when no cache-control max-age directive is present, default validity is 604800 seconds (one week) added to the reception time in milliseconds.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please check start.md or the test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/.

feature1_1_construct.json.description: Constructing a cache record from a source URL, optionally with an explicit key. When no key is supplied the record's key defaults to the URL; when an explicit key is supplied it is kept verbatim. The URL is always retained as given.

feature1_2_deserialize.json.description: Reconstructing a cache record from a persisted flat key/value record loaded from storage. Recognised fields are the numeric id, the URL, an optional key, the relative file path, the entity tag, and the validity timestamp stored as epoch milliseconds. When the record omits the key field the key falls back to the URL; when present it is used directly. The validity timestamp is reported back as epoch milliseconds.

cases.expected_output:
id=3
url=baseflow.com/test.png
key=baseflow.com/test.png
relativePath=test.png
eTag=test1
validTill_millis=1585301160000
length=null

id=3
url=baseflow.com/test.png
key=testId1234
relativePath=test.png
eTag=test1
validTill_millis=1585301160000
length=null

feature1_3_serialize.json.description: Serialising a cache record into a flat key/value record suitable for persistence. The validity timestamp is written as epoch milliseconds (or 0 when absent) and a 'touched' timestamp is stamped from the current clock at serialisation time. The id is only emitted when the record has one; the remaining identifying fields (url, key, relative path, entity tag, length) are written through unchanged.

cases.expected_output:
url=baseflow.com/test.png
key=testKey1234
relativePath=test.png
eTag=test1
validTill=1585301160000
touched=1585387560000
length=null
id=3

feature1_4_copy.json.description: Producing a modified copy of a cache record by overriding a single field while leaving the others intact. Any one of url, id, relative path, validity timestamp, entity tag, or length may be replaced; fields not mentioned are carried over from the original. The key is always preserved from the original record and is never altered by a copy.

cases.expected_output:
id=1
url=www.test.com/image
key=test123
relativePath=test.png
eTag=test1
validTill_millis=1585387560000
length=200

id=1
url=www.someotherurl.com
key=test123
relativePath=test.png
eTag=test1
validTill_millis=1585387560000
length=200

feature2_1_response_metadata.json.description: Extracting cache metadata from an HTTP response to a file request. The reported metadata is the HTTP status code, the content length, the entity tag (from the response's etag header, or null when absent), the file extension derived from the content type, and the validity timestamp. When the response carries a 'max-age' cache directive the content is valid for that many seconds from the moment the response is received; the validity timestamp is reported as epoch milliseconds relative to the supplied reception time.

feature2_2_file_extension.json.description: Deriving the on-disk file extension from an HTTP response's content type. A recognised media type maps to its conventional extension; an unrecognised media type falls back to a dot followed by the content subtype. Parameters after the media type (such as a charset) are ignored when determining the extension. With no cache directive present the content defaults to a one-week validity measured from the reception time.

feature3_progress.json.description: Computing fractional download progress from the total expected size and the number of bytes downloaded so far. Progress is reported as the ratio of downloaded bytes to total size (a value between 0 and 1), alongside the raw downloaded count and total size.
